Each sensor opens a TCP socket to the metadata DB that never closes properly afterward
Each sensor doubles the storage cost of the metadata database for the duration of the wait
Each sensor crashes the scheduler when its timeout fires before the upstream DAG finishes
questions.correctAnswer: Each sensor occupies a worker slot during its wait — many sensors exhaust the worker pool
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: sensors hold slots. Use Datasets where possible, reschedule mode (not poke) for any remaining sensors, and a dedicated pool to cap them.

questions.question: Lesson 10 names four scheduling mechanisms in SaulėGrid's architecture. Which set is correct?
options: Cron for chain entry points, Datasets for cross-DAG, timetables for calendars, API triggers for ad-hoc
Cron for everything in production, Datasets for staging, sensors for legacy, plugins for custom logic
Datasets for all DAGs, manual triggers for emergencies, timetables for cron, plugins for everything else
Cron for hourly DAGs, Datasets for daily DAGs, timetables for weekly DAGs, sensors for monthly DAGs
questions.correctAnswer: Cron for chain entry points, Datasets for cross-DAG, timetables for calendars, API triggers for ad-hoc
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: each mechanism has a specific purpose. No DAG depends on a time-buffer assumption — that is the whole point of the architecture.

questions.question: What problem do custom hooks and operators solve that copy-pasted task code does not?
options: A bug fix in one component propagates to every DAG that uses it — single point of change
A custom operator runs faster than a function because it bypasses Python's function-call overhead
A custom operator is exempt from Airflow's parsing budget so heavy imports become free at runtime
A custom operator can be scheduled independently of the DAG that contains it for resilience
questions.correctAnswer: A bug fix in one component propagates to every DAG that uses it — single point of change
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: copy-paste = bug fixes drift across copies. Components = one fix, every DAG benefits. The shift from 'write the logic' to 'build the component'.

questions.question: What does SaulėGrid's custom hook convention require every interaction method to do?
options: Return a metadata dictionary with status, duration, and domain-specific fields for monitoring
Return the raw response object exactly as the underlying provider library returned it to the caller
Return None and rely on the operator's execute method to construct any metadata for the task
Return a string formatted as 'OK' on success or 'FAIL: <reason>' on any kind of failure
questions.correctAnswer: Return a metadata dictionary with status, duration, and domain-specific fields for monitoring
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: consistent metadata structure means dashboards can aggregate hook performance across DAGs without custom parsing for each one.

questions.question: What does `template_fields = ('data_date', 'remote_path', 'bronze_path')` do on a custom operator?
options: Marks those fields for Jinja templating at task runtime — supports `{{ ds }}` substitution
Locks those fields as read-only after instantiation so nothing can modify them at runtime
Renames those fields automatically by replacing underscores with hyphens before serialization
Hides those fields from the Airflow UI so credentials embedded in them are not exposed
questions.correctAnswer: Marks those fields for Jinja templating at task runtime — supports `{{ ds }}` substitution
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: template_fields lists which constructor parameters Airflow renders through Jinja at execution time, enabling `{{ ds }}` and other macros.

questions.question: Why must `__init__` on a custom operator be lightweight?
options: The scheduler calls __init__ on every parse cycle to build the task graph — heavy work runs every 30s
The metadata database serializes __init__ once per DAG Run and caches the result for the executor
The web server invokes __init__ on every page load to render the operator's UI configuration
Airflow blocks __init__ that exceeds 100ms because it freezes the scheduler's main event loop
questions.correctAnswer: The scheduler calls __init__ on every parse cycle to build the task graph — heavy work runs every 30s
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: __init__ runs on every DAG parse. Heavy imports or DB calls in __init__ slow the entire installation. Defer heavy work to execute().

questions.question: When should you use a Task Group inside a DAG?
options: When the DAG has 8+ tasks that fall into clear logical sub-units worth collapsing in the Graph View
When the DAG has 3-5 tasks of any kind because Task Groups are mandatory for production DAGs
When the DAG uses sensors so each sensor is grouped with the task that depends on its result
questions.correctAnswer: When the DAG has 8+ tasks that fall into clear logical sub-units worth collapsing in the Graph View
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: groups visually organize related tasks. For 3-5 flat tasks, groups add unnecessary nesting; for 8+ tasks across domains, groups reveal structure.

questions.question: Why does SaulėGrid use the indirect-passing pattern for large XCom values?
options: Large data goes to S3 with the path passed via XCom — keeps the metadata DB small and fast
Large data is split into 100KB XCom chunks so the metadata DB can spread load across rows
Large data is cached in Redis with the cache key passed via XCom for cross-task reuse
Large data is gzipped before being pushed to XCom so the database compression saves bytes
questions.correctAnswer: Large data goes to S3 with the path passed via XCom — keeps the metadata DB small and fast
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: XCom is for small values (paths, counts, flags). Large values bloat the metadata DB and slow every scheduler query.

questions.question: What is the architectural advantage of a custom XCom backend (e.g., S3XComBackend) over the indirect-passing convention?
options: Platform-level enforcement — large values go to S3 transparently, even if developers forget
Better serialization — the backend uses Protocol Buffers instead of JSON for compactness
Lower latency — the backend caches XCom values in Redis to skip the metadata DB altogether
Encryption — the backend encrypts XCom values at rest using a separate AWS KMS key
questions.correctAnswer: Platform-level enforcement — large values go to S3 transparently, even if developers forget
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: convention requires every developer to remember it. Backend = platform guardrail that cannot be forgotten or bypassed.

questions.question: When should you use `@task.short_circuit` instead of `@task.branch`?
options: Pre-condition check that should skip ALL downstream tasks when False (binary go/no-go)
Choosing between 2-4 distinct downstream paths based on a value computed by the task
Wrapping a Python function so it runs in an isolated virtualenv with pinned requirements
Reusing a multi-task pattern across 3+ DAGs without copy-pasting the task definitions
questions.correctAnswer: Pre-condition check that should skip ALL downstream tasks when False (binary go/no-go)
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: short_circuit returns True (continue) or False (skip everything downstream). Branch picks one path among several. Different shapes of decision.

questions.question: SaulėGrid's rule: a function is promoted to `saulegrid_common` only after it is used in 3+ DAGs. Why?
options: Prevents the shared package from becoming a dumping ground for every utility function
Three uses are required by Python's packaging guidelines for any code shared between modules
Airflow's scheduler caches the package only when at least three DAGs reference it concurrently
Three uses guarantee statistical significance when measuring the function's average runtime
questions.correctAnswer: Prevents the shared package from becoming a dumping ground for every utility function
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: shared package = team API contract. Promoting too eagerly = a dumping ground. The 3-DAG threshold proves cross-DAG value first.

questions.question: Lesson 10's production Bronze DAG is 18 lines. Where did the complexity go?
options: Encapsulated in shared hooks, operators, and Datasets in saulegrid_common — DAG composes them
Compressed via Airflow's built-in DAG minifier that runs during the deployment Docker build
Spread across helper modules in the dags/ folder that the scheduler loads alongside the DAG file
Removed entirely — modern Airflow handles SFTP and validation through its built-in providers
questions.correctAnswer: Encapsulated in shared hooks, operators, and Datasets in saulegrid_common — DAG composes them
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: components live in saulegrid_common. The DAG composes them. A bug fix in any component fixes every DAG that uses it.

questions.question: Why is idempotency described as the prerequisite for every other pattern in Module 3?
options: Retries, recovery, and dead-letter handling all assume re-running a task produces the same result
Idempotency was added to Airflow before retries and the codebase still requires it for compatibility
The scheduler refuses to schedule a task without an idempotent flag set in the operator class
Idempotency is required by SaulėGrid's compliance auditors for every pipeline that touches billing
questions.correctAnswer: Retries, recovery, and dead-letter handling all assume re-running a task produces the same result
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: incremental processing assumes safe reprocessing. Retries assume retries are safe. SLA recovery assumes clearing+rerun produces correct output. Everything builds on idempotency.

questions.question: SaulėGrid's idempotency convention by layer is which combination?
options: Bronze: overwrite. Silver: delete-then-write the date partition. Gold: upsert by composite key.
Bronze: append for raw data. Silver: append for transformed data. Gold: full refresh nightly.
Bronze: upsert by source URI. Silver: overwrite the entire table. Gold: insert with timestamp suffix.
Bronze: insert and never delete. Silver: delete and never insert. Gold: only update existing rows.
questions.correctAnswer: Bronze: overwrite. Silver: delete-then-write the date partition. Gold: upsert by composite key.
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: each layer's write strategy matches its data shape — Bronze raw, Silver partitioned, Gold relational with concurrent reads.

questions.question: What does the watermark pattern in Lesson 3 use Variables for?
options: Tracking the last processed timestamp so the next run reads only newer records
Tracking which DAG Run was most recent so backfills know where to resume from
Tracking the SLA threshold for the pipeline so the run can adjust its retry behavior
Tracking the worker count so the autoscaler knows how many workers the DAG needs
questions.correctAnswer: Tracking the last processed timestamp so the next run reads only newer records
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: watermark = 'I have processed up to timestamp X'. Next run reads records with timestamp > X. Append after the watermark advances.

questions.question: SaulėGrid's tiered retry strategy uses different settings per system. Why does the SFTP download use long delays with exponential backoff?
options: The provider rate-limits aggressive reconnections — exponential backoff avoids triggering the limit
SFTP servers cannot handle more than one retry per minute due to the protocol's session limits
Exponential backoff is required by RFC 3617 for any SFTP-based file transfer in production
The provider charges per connection attempt and exponential backoff minimizes the bill
questions.correctAnswer: The provider rate-limits aggressive reconnections — exponential backoff avoids triggering the limit
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: encode the operational reality — SFTP rate-limits, weather APIs recover quickly, databases need fixed intervals. One-size-fits-all retries are wrong.

questions.question: What does an `sla_miss_callback` on a DAG actually do?
options: Fires a custom function when a task with `sla=...` completes after its threshold — sends alerts
Cancels the DAG Run when its first task exceeds the SLA so resources go to other DAGs
Reschedules the DAG Run to a later slot when the SLA cannot be met within the current window
Logs the SLA miss to a dedicated table for the regulator's quarterly compliance audit only
questions.correctAnswer: Fires a custom function when a task with `sla=...` completes after its threshold — sends alerts
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: SLA = time relative to logical_date by which a task must complete. Miss callback runs your custom alert/escalation logic when breached.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a dead-letter table in SaulėGrid's Silver pipeline?
options: Capture rejected records with rejection_reason and code so they can be investigated and reprocessed
Hold all records that the scheduler could not parse during DAG file scanning at startup
Store the most recent SLA miss for every Gold-layer DAG indexed by the breach severity
Buffer raw Bronze data during pipeline maintenance windows for catchup processing later
questions.correctAnswer: Capture rejected records with rejection_reason and code so they can be investigated and reprocessed
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: dead-letter = quality gate's reject pile. Each rejection has a code (NULL_METER_ID, etc.) for monitoring and selective reprocessing.

questions.question: After a Saturday Bronze failure, what is the recovery sequence Lesson 7 prescribes?
options: Clear the failed run with downstream, then trigger Sunday's run, then trigger Monday's run
Backfill the entire month from start_date to today with --reset-dagruns set on every DAG
Pause every dependent DAG, manually copy missing files, and unpause them once verification completes
Delete the failed DAG Run from the metadata database and let the next scheduled run catch up alone
questions.correctAnswer: Clear the failed run with downstream, then trigger Sunday's run, then trigger Monday's run
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: clear = re-evaluate dependencies and reset state. Then trigger the missed dates one by one so Datasets cascade properly through Silver and Gold.

questions.question: What mechanism guarantees cross-DAG consistency in SaulėGrid's architecture?
options: Dataset scheduling — downstream DAGs run only when upstream data is genuinely ready
ExternalTaskSensors — every Silver DAG polls every Bronze DAG until completion
Manual triggers — a senior engineer kicks off each layer once the previous one is verified
Cron with generous time buffers — a 90-minute gap between layers absorbs any drift
questions.correctAnswer: Dataset scheduling — downstream DAGs run only when upstream data is genuinely ready
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: Datasets are the consistency mechanism by default. Freshness monitoring is the verification layer that detects gaps the scheduler did not prevent.

questions.question: Lesson 9 says some DAG changes are 'irreversible.' Which of these is irreversible?
options: Removing a column from the output schema — downstream consumers referencing it will break
Adding a nullable column to the output schema — downstream queries that ignore it keep working
Adding a new validation rule — rejected records go to dead-letter and can be reprocessed
Switching the schedule from cron to Datasets — the scheduler keeps both forms during migration
questions.correctAnswer: Removing a column from the output schema — downstream consumers referencing it will break
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: irreversible = downstream consumers depend on the prior state. Removing a column or changing a partition key cannot be quietly undone.

questions.question: SaulėGrid's pipeline design checklist enforces at least eight standards. Which one is correct?
options: Idempotency, write strategy, incremental scope, retry config, SLA defined, dead letters, recovery runbook, Dataset scheduling
Async only, no XCom, no retries, no SLAs, no dead letters, no documentation, no version control, no review
DAG count under five, line count under one hundred, single-task DAGs, no shared package, no tests, fast parse
Branching everywhere, sensors required, cron only, manual deployment, manual rotation, Admin role for all
questions.correctAnswer: Idempotency, write strategy, incremental scope, retry config, SLA defined, dead letters, recovery runbook, Dataset scheduling
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: the checklist codifies the module's lessons into a PR review template. Each unchecked item is a risk to investigate before merge.

questions.question: Why is DAG code described as 'executor-agnostic'?
options: The same DAG runs on LocalExecutor in dev and CeleryExecutor in prod without modification
The executor field is read at parse time and the DAG cannot be migrated to another executor later
DAG code locks itself to one executor at startup so the scheduler cannot reroute mid-execution
Each executor type requires its own DAG file format and the codebase must maintain three copies
questions.correctAnswer: The same DAG runs on LocalExecutor in dev and CeleryExecutor in prod without modification
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: executor is an infrastructure decision, not a code decision. Scheduler/DB/web server work identically; the executor is the only swappable piece.

questions.question: When is LocalExecutor the right choice for SaulėGrid?
options: Single machine with parallel tasks where the workload fits in one host's CPU/memory budget
Distributed cluster across data centers where Redis brokers handle inter-machine messaging
Kubernetes deployment where each task gets its own pod and resource quota at runtime
Edge deployment with intermittent connectivity where tasks queue and replay on reconnect
questions.correctAnswer: Single machine with parallel tasks where the workload fits in one host's CPU/memory budget
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: LocalExecutor parallelizes processes on one machine. No broker, no workers, minimal operational overhead. Right until the host outgrows the workload.

questions.question: What architectural advantage does CeleryExecutor have over LocalExecutor?
options: The message broker decouples scheduler from workers — workers can be added without scheduler restart
The broker stores task results permanently so workers can read other workers' outputs over the network
The broker enforces strict ordering across all workers so tasks complete in deterministic sequence
The broker eliminates the need for a metadata database by storing all task state in Redis directly
questions.correctAnswer: The message broker decouples scheduler from workers — workers can be added without scheduler restart
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: broker = decoupling layer. Add/remove workers without restart. Each worker has its own resources — no contention with the scheduler host.

questions.question: When does KubernetesExecutor's per-task pod model become the right trade-off?
options: When tasks need different Python dependencies that would conflict on a shared worker host
When pipelines must process data faster than CeleryExecutor can dispatch tasks to its workers
When the team wants to eliminate the metadata database by relying on Kubernetes etcd instead
When the schedule is sub-minute and Kubernetes is the only executor that supports rapid dispatch
questions.correctAnswer: When tasks need different Python dependencies that would conflict on a shared worker host
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: pod-per-task = different Docker images per task. Trades CeleryExecutor's simplicity for dependency isolation. Worth it only if isolation is genuinely needed.

questions.question: SaulėGrid's executor selection principle is which of these?
options: Choose the simplest executor that meets current requirements with 12-18 months of growth headroom
Always choose KubernetesExecutor because it is the most powerful and future-proof of the three options
Always choose CeleryExecutor because Redis is the de-facto standard message broker for orchestration
Always choose LocalExecutor because the operational overhead of distributed executors is never justified
questions.correctAnswer: Choose the simplest executor that meets current requirements with 12-18 months of growth headroom
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: the right executor is the one the team can debug at 2 AM. Power without operational fluency is a liability, not an asset.

questions.question: Why does SaulėGrid create dedicated pools like `sftp_pool` (2 slots) and `warehouse_pool` (5 slots)?
options: Each pool's slot count matches the external system's documented concurrency limit
Each pool is required by Airflow before any task that connects to an external system can run
Each pool replaces the executor's parallelism setting for tasks that target external systems
Each pool acts as a load balancer that distributes work across multiple identical external systems
questions.correctAnswer: Each pool's slot count matches the external system's documented concurrency limit
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: pools enforce external-system concurrency limits. The provider allows 2 SFTP connections; the pool guarantees Airflow respects that.

questions.question: What happens when a task is assigned `queue='gpu'` but no worker subscribes to the gpu queue?
options: The task sits in the queue indefinitely — not failed, not timed out, just waiting forever
The scheduler reassigns the task to the default queue after a 60-second timeout window
The web server displays a red 'orphaned queue' badge in the UI's task instance view
Airflow raises an OrphanedQueueException at parse time and the DAG fails to load entirely
questions.correctAnswer: The task sits in the queue indefinitely — not failed, not timed out, just waiting forever
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: orphaned queue = silent indefinite wait. SaulėGrid's CI check validates queue assignments against the active worker pool to prevent this.

questions.question: SaulėGrid's worker scaling is described as 'three-layer hybrid'. Which layers?
options: Persistent workers + scheduled additional workers + Celery autoscale within each worker
Hot standby workers + cold backup workers + KEDA-based Kubernetes scaling on top
On-prem workers + cloud burst workers + edge workers near the data sources around the EU
questions.correctAnswer: Persistent workers + scheduled additional workers + Celery autoscale within each worker
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: persistent (always-on baseline) + scheduled (cron starts overnight workers) + autoscale (burst). Each layer covers a different demand pattern.

questions.question: What is a 'zombie task' in Airflow's executor model?
options: A running task whose worker process stopped sending heartbeats — scheduler marks it failed
A queued task whose worker started running it but never reported the new state to the database
A succeeded task whose XCom value was lost before the executor could persist it to storage
questions.correctAnswer: A running task whose worker process stopped sending heartbeats — scheduler marks it failed
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: zombie = task says it's running but the worker is gone (crashed, network partition, etc.). After scheduler_zombie_task_threshold seconds, scheduler fails it.

questions.question: Lesson 10 describes SaulėGrid's runtime architecture. Which combination matches?
options: CeleryExecutor + Redis with Sentinel + 3 persistent workers + 2 burst workers + queue specialization
KubernetesExecutor + etcd + per-task pods + horizontal pod autoscaler + namespace isolation
LocalExecutor + SQLite + single host + cron-based DAG dispatch + standalone setup
questions.correctAnswer: CeleryExecutor + Redis with Sentinel + 3 persistent workers + 2 burst workers + queue specialization
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: their actual production deployment. CeleryExecutor for distributed capacity, Redis Sentinel for HA broker, RDS PostgreSQL for the metadata DB.

questions.question: Why does manual UI-based connection management break down at SaulėGrid's scale (12 connections × 3 environments)?
options: Manual entry across environments is error-prone — a wrong-pasted password causes silent runtime failures
The Airflow UI rejects any connection containing more than 12 characters in its password field
Connections defined in the UI are not exported during backups so they are lost on host failure
The metadata database limits connection records to 36 entries before the UI starts truncating them
questions.correctAnswer: Manual entry across environments is error-prone — a wrong-pasted password causes silent runtime failures
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: manual = error-prone. Wrong-pasted password = silent runtime failure. Secrets backends + config-as-code replace human typing with automation.

questions.question: What is the connection lookup chain when a Secrets Backend is configured?
options: Secrets Backend → environment variables → metadata database — first hit wins
Metadata database → Secrets Backend → environment variables — UI takes priority
Environment variables → metadata database → Secrets Backend — local overrides win
Secrets Backend only — environment variables and database are ignored when configured
questions.correctAnswer: Secrets Backend → environment variables → metadata database — first hit wins
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: backend has highest priority. This allows gradual migration — move connections to Vault one at a time without removing them from the database.

questions.question: What is the most common configuration error when setting up Vault as a Secrets Backend?
options: Path mismatch — KV v2 silently inserts a 'data' segment that backend_kwargs must account for
Auth mismatch — AppRole tokens expire after 60 seconds and the backend cannot refresh them
TLS mismatch — Vault requires TLS 1.3 but Airflow's HTTP client defaults to TLS 1.2 always
Encoding mismatch — Vault stores secrets as UTF-16 but Airflow expects UTF-8 throughout
questions.correctAnswer: Path mismatch — KV v2 silently inserts a 'data' segment that backend_kwargs must account for
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: KV v2 stores at secret/data/{path} but the CLI hides this. backend_kwargs must align mount_point + path with the actual storage layout.

questions.question: What is the credential management hierarchy from Lesson 4 (best to worst)?
options: Eliminate (IAM roles) → Generate dynamically (OAuth tokens) → Store in Vault (static passwords)
Hardcode in DAGs → Store in metadata DB → Store in Vault → Store in encrypted env vars
Generate per-task → Cache in Redis → Replicate across workers → Sync to the metadata DB
Static passwords → Rotated quarterly → Stored in metadata DB → Encrypted with team-shared key
questions.correctAnswer: Eliminate (IAM roles) → Generate dynamically (OAuth tokens) → Store in Vault (static passwords)
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: zero credentials > per-task tokens > stored secrets. Each step up reduces the surface area for credential incidents.

questions.question: Why is `bronze_path = Variable.get('saulegrid.bronze.path')` at the top of a DAG file dangerous?
options: It queries the metadata database on every scheduler parse — many queries per minute total
It locks the variable for the DAG and prevents other DAGs from reading the same value
It caches the value forever and ignores any updates the operator makes through the UI
It crashes the scheduler when the variable is missing instead of using a default value
questions.correctAnswer: It queries the metadata database on every scheduler parse — many queries per minute total
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: top-level code runs every parse. Variable.get() at module level = constant DB hammering. Move it inside task callables.

questions.question: What is SaulėGrid's 'configuration as code' principle?
options: YAML files in Git are the source of truth — the UI displays current state, the repo wins on drift
All configuration lives in the Airflow UI — Git just holds DAG code and never touches connections
Configuration is generated at deploy time by an LLM that reads the README and infers the right values
Each engineer maintains their own local YAML and the scheduler picks the most recently modified file
questions.correctAnswer: YAML files in Git are the source of truth — the UI displays current state, the repo wins on drift
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: Git wins. The init script runs on every deploy and corrects any UI drift back to the repo's intent.

questions.question: What is SaulėGrid's variable naming convention?
options: saulegrid.{domain}.{key} — namespaced prefix prevents collisions and groups related variables
ALL_CAPS_WITH_UNDERSCORES — required by the metadata DB's case-sensitive lookup index
{environment}-{key} — environment prefix prevents dev variables from leaking into production
v1_{key} — version prefix lets the team add v2_{key} without breaking existing DAG references
questions.correctAnswer: saulegrid.{domain}.{key} — namespaced prefix prevents collisions and groups related variables
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: namespaced names. Listing 'saulegrid.bronze.*' shows all bronze-related vars; 'saulegrid.*' shows the entire team's configuration.

questions.question: Why does SaulėGrid synchronize its credential rotation schedule with the provider's rotation cycle?
options: It eliminates the risk of using expired credentials — the update happens on the day the new credential becomes available
It satisfies a Lithuanian regulatory requirement that every Vault operation align with the provider's calendar
It guarantees that two providers never rotate on the same day so the platform team is never overwhelmed
It reduces the Vault audit log size because synchronized writes compress better than scattered ones
questions.correctAnswer: It eliminates the risk of using expired credentials — the update happens on the day the new credential becomes available
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: provider issues new credential on day X → SaulėGrid updates Vault on day X. No expired-credential window.

questions.question: What does the connection registry (config/connection_registry.yaml) document?
options: Owner, rotation schedule, Vault path, consuming DAGs, and last-verified date for each connection
Every connection's password history with timestamps so the team can roll back to any prior version
The full URI of every connection in plaintext so engineers can copy-paste into local development
Each connection's TCP port assignment so the network team can update firewall rules in advance
questions.correctAnswer: Owner, rotation schedule, Vault path, consuming DAGs, and last-verified date for each connection
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: registry = operational metadata. Audit task compares it against the metadata DB to find undocumented or missing connections.

questions.question: Lesson 10's configuration architecture has four layers. Which list matches?
options: DAG code (Git) + Configuration (Variables/YAML) + Credentials (Vault) + Operational metadata (registry)
Local config + staging config + production config + DR config — each environment is fully self-contained
Hardcoded values + manual overrides + UI tweaks + cron-injected secrets — the legacy four-step process
API keys + OAuth tokens + IAM roles + service principals — the four authentication mechanisms in use
questions.correctAnswer: DAG code (Git) + Configuration (Variables/YAML) + Credentials (Vault) + Operational metadata (registry)
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: separation of concerns across four layers, each with a defined source of truth and deployment target.

questions.question: How is testing DAGs different from testing application code?
options: DAG tests verify both the orchestration platform's ability to parse/schedule and the logic correctness
DAG tests are slower because every test must spin up a full Airflow scheduler and a Postgres database
DAG tests cannot use mocks because Airflow forbids replacing any Connection or Hook at test runtime
DAG tests only run during deployment because the test runner cannot resolve Airflow imports locally
questions.correctAnswer: DAG tests verify both the orchestration platform's ability to parse/schedule and the logic correctness
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: app tests = function correctness. DAG tests = both 'can Airflow run this?' and 'does the logic produce correct output?' Two layers, two test types.

questions.question: What does `DagBag(dag_folder='dags/')` plus `assert dag_bag.import_errors == {}` catch?
options: Circular imports, syntax errors, missing dependencies, and exceptions in top-level DAG code
Slow database queries, oversized XCom values, and credentials accidentally stored in the DAG file
Schedule misconfigurations such as catchup=True with a far-past start_date and no max_active_runs
Worker imbalance, queue assignment errors, and pool exhaustion across the executor's slot pool
questions.correctAnswer: Circular imports, syntax errors, missing dependencies, and exceptions in top-level DAG code
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: a single integrity test catches every structural error class. The cheapest, highest-value test in the whole suite.

questions.question: Structure validation tests verify which kinds of conventions?
options: Naming patterns, required tags, SLA on Gold DAGs, retries on external-system tasks, access control
Algorithmic correctness of business logic functions used inside the DAG's transformation tasks
Network-level latency between the scheduler host and external systems referenced by Connections
Memory consumption of each Airflow worker process during a representative production workload
questions.correctAnswer: Naming patterns, required tags, SLA on Gold DAGs, retries on external-system tasks, access control
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: structure = conventions like dag_{layer}_{domain}, snake_case task IDs, every Gold DAG has sla_miss_callback. The executable specification of team conventions.

questions.question: Why does SaulėGrid keep transformation logic in `saulegrid_common/transformations/` separate from `dags/`?
options: Pure functions with no Airflow imports can be unit-tested directly without any Airflow runtime
Pure functions are required by Python's packaging spec to live outside the dags/ folder by name
Pure functions cannot use type hints so keeping them separate prevents type-checking conflicts
Pure functions are slower to import so isolating them prevents the scheduler from loading them
questions.correctAnswer: Pure functions with no Airflow imports can be unit-tested directly without any Airflow runtime
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: thin DAG = call business function. Business function = pure logic, easy to unit test. Tests run in milliseconds without Airflow.

questions.question: What does SaulėGrid's mocking boundary look like across test layers?
options: Unit/structure: mock everything. Integration: Docker containers + mock servers. Staging: real endpoints.
Unit: real DB. Integration: real DB. Staging: real DB. Same boundary throughout for consistency.
Unit: mock SFTP only. Integration: mock everything. Staging: real Postgres but mocked APIs.
Unit: full Airflow runtime. Integration: full Airflow runtime. Staging: production Airflow runtime.
questions.correctAnswer: Unit/structure: mock everything. Integration: Docker containers + mock servers. Staging: real endpoints.
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: each layer catches different failure classes. Unit fast and reliable; integration realistic; staging high-confidence end-to-end.

questions.question: What does `dag.test()` provide for development?
options: Quick local DAG execution in test mode — sequential, no scheduler, with optional connection overrides
Continuous DAG monitoring across staging and production with automatic test result aggregation
Auto-generation of integration tests from a recorded DAG Run — captures inputs and replays them
A REST endpoint that triggers the DAG with test fixtures and reports the result via webhook
questions.correctAnswer: Quick local DAG execution in test mode — sequential, no scheduler, with optional connection overrides
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: dag.test() runs the DAG locally without scheduler/executor. Quick check during development; not a CI tool.

questions.question: What is a 'dirty fixture' in SaulėGrid's data quality testing?
options: CSV fixture with intentional quality issues per record + comment explaining the expected rejection
CSV fixture pulled directly from production and stored unchanged for the test suite to use
CSV fixture that has been corrupted by a fuzzing tool to test the validator's exception handling
CSV fixture that contains personal data anonymized by the team for staging-only data quality tests
questions.correctAnswer: CSV fixture with intentional quality issues per record + comment explaining the expected rejection
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: dirty fixture = known-bad records, each with a comment naming the expected rejection code. Distribution test verifies every code is exercised.

questions.question: What is SaulėGrid's testing pyramid execution time budget for the per-push CI run?
options: Under 90 seconds total — fast enough to be a natural part of the development workflow
Under 10 minutes total — long enough to run extensive end-to-end tests for every push
Under 5 seconds total — tests must run faster than the scheduler's heartbeat interval
Under 30 minutes total — comprehensive coverage requires running the full integration suite
questions.correctAnswer: Under 90 seconds total — fast enough to be a natural part of the development workflow
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: 90s keeps CI in the development workflow. Slower CI = developers batch changes and skip CI locally — defeating the purpose.

questions.question: What does SaulėGrid's test environment principle say?
options: Every test runnable on a fresh machine with only Python and Docker installed — no other setup
Every test reuses the staging Airflow instance to share connections, variables, and pools globally
Every test requires a senior engineer's machine because the local setup needs production secrets
Every test pulls fixtures from S3 at runtime so the repository stays small and easy to clone
questions.correctAnswer: Every test runnable on a fresh machine with only Python and Docker installed — no other setup
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: clone repo + pip install + docker-compose up = full test suite running. New team member's first day = productive.

questions.question: Lesson 10 says SaulėGrid's testing standards transform a question. From what to what?
options: From 'will this DAG work in production?' to 'this DAG is verified by 123 assertions — it will'
From 'who tested this DAG?' to 'the senior engineer who reviewed the PR personally tested it'
From 'when was the last test run?' to 'every commit triggers the full test suite automatically'
From 'are tests required?' to 'the team writes tests when they have time after the deadline'
questions.correctAnswer: From 'will this DAG work in production?' to 'this DAG is verified by 123 assertions — it will'
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: testing transforms uncertainty into verified confidence. The pyramid covers structure → logic → integration → idempotency.

questions.question: What is wrong with manual `scp dags/*.py airflow-prod:/opt/airflow/dags/` deployments?
options: No test validation, no deployment record, no protection against simultaneous pushes, no rollback mechanism
scp is slower than rsync so deployments take twice as long for the same set of DAG files
scp does not preserve file permissions and the scheduler then refuses to load the new DAG files
scp blocks the scheduler for the duration of the copy and pauses every running DAG Run
questions.correctAnswer: No test validation, no deployment record, no protection against simultaneous pushes, no rollback mechanism
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: manual = quality depends on the human's attention on that day. Automated = quality is the pipeline's configuration, reviewed and consistent.

questions.question: Why does SaulėGrid pin every dependency in requirements.txt to an exact version?
options: Reproducibility — the same versions install in dev, staging, and production every single time
Performance — pinned versions skip the dependency resolver and pip install completes faster
Security — pinned versions cannot receive security patches and stay at the audited release
Licensing — pinned versions guarantee the legal team approved every dependency by name
questions.correctAnswer: Reproducibility — the same versions install in dev, staging, and production every single time
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: pinning eliminates 'works on my machine' surprises. Quarterly upgrade cycle moves pins forward deliberately.

questions.question: Why does SaulėGrid target a 90-second CI pipeline?
options: Fast enough to be a natural part of the workflow — slower CI = developers skip it locally
Slower than 90s would exceed GitHub Actions' free-tier compute budget for the team's repository
90s is the maximum interval Airflow's scheduler tolerates between deploys without restarting
90s lets the team run CI on every keystroke without rate-limiting from the GitHub API
questions.correctAnswer: Fast enough to be a natural part of the workflow — slower CI = developers skip it locally
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: CI must be a partner, not a bottleneck. If CI is slow, devs batch changes and break the fast-feedback loop.

questions.question: What does SaulėGrid's CI check for deprecated patterns like `schedule_interval=`?
options: Scans every DAG file for deprecated keywords and fails the build with the suggested replacement
Auto-rewrites the deprecated keyword to the modern form and creates a follow-up PR for review
Adds a warning comment to the PR but does not block the merge if other tests pass cleanly
Logs the deprecation to the metadata database for the quarterly compliance audit by the regulator
questions.correctAnswer: Scans every DAG file for deprecated keywords and fails the build with the suggested replacement
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: CI catches deprecated APIs (schedule_interval, execution_date, provide_context, SubDagOperator) before they reach production.

questions.question: How does SaulėGrid mitigate deploying DAG changes while tasks are running?
options: Deploy only during the 10:00-14:00 quiet window when no DAG Runs are typically in progress
Pause every running DAG Run before deploy and resume them after deployment completes successfully
Wait until the metadata database is empty so the deploy has zero in-flight task instances
Deploy to a new Airflow instance and switch traffic with DNS — old instance drains over hours
questions.correctAnswer: Deploy only during the 10:00-14:00 quiet window when no DAG Runs are typically in progress
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: 10:00-14:00 = scheduled DAGs have completed. Running tasks complete with old code; next runs use new code — generally safe.

questions.question: What does SaulėGrid's environment promotion model look like?
options: Dev → Staging is automatic on merge. Staging → Production is manual after a validation checklist.
Dev → Staging → Production all automatic on merge with no human intervention or validation steps.
Dev → Production directly because staging is too expensive to maintain for a small platform team.
Production runs manually-deployed code; dev/staging are continuously deployed for testing only.
questions.correctAnswer: Dev → Staging is automatic on merge. Staging → Production is manual after a validation checklist.
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: automation up to staging, human gate before production. Validation checklist + team approval prevents bad code from auto-deploying to billing.

questions.question: Why does SaulėGrid rehearse rollback procedures quarterly?
options: An untested rollback is hope, not a procedure — practice makes the 15-minute target achievable
Quarterly rehearsal is required by Lithuanian financial regulations for billing-critical infrastructure
Quarterly rehearsal generates audit log entries that the security team needs for compliance reports
Quarterly rehearsal lets the team practice writing post-mortems without an actual incident occurring
questions.correctAnswer: An untested rollback is hope, not a procedure — practice makes the 15-minute target achievable
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: untested = unproven. Quarterly drills make every team member fluent in `helm rollback` so the safety net works under real pressure.

questions.question: How does SaulėGrid handle a security CVE in a transitive dependency?
options: Compressed timeline — assess severity, update the package, run tests, 24-hour staging, deploy
Wait for the next quarterly upgrade cycle so the change goes through the standard review process
Pin the vulnerable package permanently and rely on network-level mitigations from the firewall team
Switch to a fork of the package that the team maintains internally with backported fixes only
questions.correctAnswer: Compressed timeline — assess severity, update the package, run tests, 24-hour staging, deploy
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: emergency patches bypass the normal one-week staging. Risk of regression < risk of exploit. SaulėGrid has shipped two such patches in 48 hours.

questions.question: Lesson 9 says SaulėGrid's infrastructure is 'cattle, not pets.' What does that mean?
options: Every component is defined in version-controlled files — the entire platform is reconstructable from Git
Every server has a unique name and is hand-configured by the platform team for its specific workload
Every host runs in a single VM image that the cloud provider auto-replaces every six months
Every Airflow component runs in a Kubernetes pod with no persistent identity across restarts
questions.correctAnswer: Every component is defined in version-controlled files — the entire platform is reconstructable from Git
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: cattle = disposable + reconstructable. Helm + Docker Compose + requirements.txt = full platform from Git. Production failure = recoverable, not catastrophic.

questions.question: Lesson 10 closes by stating what guarantee the deployment pipeline now provides?
options: Every change is tested, reviewed, traceable, and reversible — the three-incident week cannot recur
Every change is automatically pushed to production within 60 seconds of being committed to main
Every change is reviewed by the entire engineering team before being merged into the main branch
Every change is logged to the metadata database for the security team's quarterly compliance audit
questions.correctAnswer: Every change is tested, reviewed, traceable, and reversible — the three-incident week cannot recur
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: automated pipeline replaces 'I deployed' with 'pipeline deployed'. The three-incident week's failure modes are structurally prevented now.

questions.question: Why does multi-team Airflow access require an explicit security model rather than implicit trust?
options: Implicit trust does not codify who can do what — the March 8 incident was a permission model failure
Implicit trust violates Lithuanian data protection regulations that require role-based authorization
Implicit trust prevents the audit log from working because Airflow has no identity to record actions to
Implicit trust crashes the web server when the user count exceeds five concurrent sessions
questions.correctAnswer: Implicit trust does not codify who can do what — the March 8 incident was a permission model failure
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: 2 engineers = trust everyone. 12 people across 3 teams = codify who can do what. The incident was a model failure, not a trust violation.

questions.question: What is SaulėGrid's policy on assigning the built-in Admin role?
options: Admin is assigned to exactly one person — the team lead. All other users get custom roles.
Admin is assigned to every full-time engineer for productivity — junior staff get the User role.
Admin is assigned to whoever is on-call this week so they can fix issues without escalating.
Admin is assigned to a shared service account that engineers use via a password rotation app.
questions.correctAnswer: Admin is assigned to exactly one person — the team lead. All other users get custom roles.
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: Admin = unrestricted. Every Admin can modify security settings, read credentials, change configuration. Limit to one person.

questions.question: What does SaulėGrid's `analytics_viewer` custom role grant?
options: Read-only access to DAGs, Task Instances, Task Logs, XCom, and Datasets — no write or trigger
Full access to Gold DAGs but no access to Bronze or Silver DAGs in any environment
Permission to trigger Gold DAGs manually so the analytics team can re-run reports on demand
Permission to modify Connection passwords so the analytics team rotates their own credentials
questions.correctAnswer: Read-only access to DAGs, Task Instances, Task Logs, XCom, and Datasets — no write or trigger
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: analytics needs to see, not change. Read-only on DAGs/instances/logs/XCom/Datasets. No edit, no trigger, no admin.

questions.question: What happens when a DAG is deployed without an `access_control` parameter?
options: The DAG is visible to all authenticated users — every role can read it by default
The DAG is hidden from all users until an Admin explicitly grants role-level permissions
The DAG fails to import — Airflow refuses to load DAGs without an access_control parameter
The DAG inherits the access_control of the most recently deployed DAG in the same DAG bag
questions.correctAnswer: The DAG is visible to all authenticated users — every role can read it by default
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: default = visible to everyone. SaulėGrid's CI test rejects PRs whose DAGs lack access_control to prevent uncontrolled visibility.

questions.question: What is SaulėGrid's authentication architecture?
options: OAuth via Google Workspace — the IdP manages identity and group→role mapping is automatic
Local username/password — credentials in the metadata DB managed by the platform admin alone
SSH keys — every engineer's public key is added to the Airflow web server's authorized_keys
API tokens only — the UI is disabled and all interaction happens through the REST API
questions.correctAnswer: OAuth via Google Workspace — the IdP manages identity and group→role mapping is automatic
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: corporate IdP manages identity. When someone leaves, deactivating their Google account immediately blocks Airflow login — no separate cleanup needed.

questions.question: Why does SaulėGrid use service account tokens (not personal credentials) for CI/CD API access?
options: Service tokens are not tied to employment status and have minimum permissions for the automation
Service tokens are 30% faster than personal credentials because they bypass the OAuth handshake
Service tokens are required by GitHub Actions because personal credentials cannot be stored as secrets
Service tokens automatically rotate every 24 hours so no manual rotation procedure is needed
questions.correctAnswer: Service tokens are not tied to employment status and have minimum permissions for the automation
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: personal creds in CI = automation breaks when developer leaves; full access if compromised. Service tokens = scoped + survives team changes.

questions.question: What is the value of audit logging according to Lesson 7?
options: Demonstrates that the security model works — verified property, not unverified assertion
Catches insider threats — most security incidents come from team members with valid access
Replaces RBAC — the audit log is granular enough to detect unauthorized actions retroactively
Identifies slow API calls — the timestamps in the log reveal performance bottlenecks per user
questions.correctAnswer: Demonstrates that the security model works — verified property, not unverified assertion
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: audit log proves RBAC works, authentication is effective, and credentials are managed. Confidence comes from verification, not assertion.

questions.question: Lesson 8 describes three network zones for SaulėGrid's Airflow deployment. Which list is correct?
options: Reverse proxy (internet-facing TLS) → Application subnet (Airflow components) → Data subnet (DB/broker)
DMZ → Public cloud → Private cloud — every component spans all three for high availability
Internal network → External network → Backup network — each handles a different traffic class
Frontend → Middleware → Backend — separation by application tier rather than by network zone
questions.correctAnswer: Reverse proxy (internet-facing TLS) → Application subnet (Airflow components) → Data subnet (DB/broker)
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: three zones with decreasing accessibility. Proxy faces internet; app subnet runs Airflow; data subnet is reachable only from the app subnet.

questions.question: Lesson 9 warns that 'security anti-patterns compound.' What does that mean operationally?
options: Fixing one anti-pattern while leaving others creates the illusion of security without the reality
Each anti-pattern multiplies the others' impact — three anti-patterns produce nine vulnerabilities total
Anti-patterns spread across DAGs over time so one infected DAG eventually corrupts every other DAG
Anti-patterns trigger compounding fines from the regulator that double for each unaddressed pattern
questions.correctAnswer: Fixing one anti-pattern while leaving others creates the illusion of security without the reality
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: partial security is often worse than none — confidence without justification. The framework has to address all patterns, not pick a few.

questions.question: Lesson 10 lists the layers of SaulėGrid's security framework. Which set?
options: Network + Authentication + RBAC + DAG-level access + Audit
Firewall + VPN + Antivirus + Patches + Backups
Encryption + Hashing + Salting + Tokens + Cookies
questions.correctAnswer: Network + Authentication + RBAC + DAG-level access + Audit
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: each layer adds a check. A request must pass all layers; the audit layer records every request regardless of allow/deny.

questions.question: What is SaulėGrid's principle for performance tuning?
options: Measure the binding constraint, apply a targeted fix, verify with the metric — never tune blindly
Increase parallelism whenever queue depth grows — it is the single lever that always helps
Switch executors when performance degrades — a different executor model fixes most issues
Add more workers continuously — extra capacity is the cheapest way to absorb performance issues
questions.correctAnswer: Measure the binding constraint, apply a targeted fix, verify with the metric — never tune blindly
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: tuning without measurement is guesswork. Scheduler loop, parse time, DB latency, queue time — these four diagnose 90% of issues.

questions.question: What does `scheduler_heartbeat_duration` measure, and what is its healthy range?
options: Total time for one scheduler loop iteration — healthy < 5 seconds, ideally < 2 seconds
Total time the scheduler spends queueing tasks — healthy < 50ms per task across the run
Total time the metadata database spends responding to scheduler queries — healthy < 1 second
Total time the executor takes to acknowledge a queued task — healthy < 100ms after enqueue
questions.correctAnswer: Total time for one scheduler loop iteration — healthy < 5 seconds, ideally < 2 seconds
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: heartbeat = full scheduler loop. > 5s = parser or DB bottleneck. Trend matters more than any single sample.

questions.question: An engineer's DAG file imports pandas at the top. What is the parse-time cost?
options: 300-500ms per parse cycle — pandas runs every 30 seconds even though no task uses it
Zero — the scheduler caches imports across parse cycles after the first heartbeat completes
5-10ms — pandas is lightweight and only meaningful for DAGs that import it dozens of times
Up to 60s — pandas conflicts with Airflow's serializer and triggers a fallback parser path
questions.correctAnswer: 300-500ms per parse cycle — pandas runs every 30 seconds even though no task uses it
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: heavy imports at module level run on every parse cycle. Defer them inside task callables — only run when the task actually executes.

questions.question: What does `.airflowignore` do, and why does SaulėGrid maintain it?
options: Excludes patterns (test files, helpers, __pycache__) from the scheduler's DAG file scan
Disables specific Airflow features that the platform team does not want exposed to engineers
Tells the metadata database which tables to skip during the weekly cleanup maintenance run
Hides flagged DAGs from the Airflow UI so the analytics team cannot see them in the list view
questions.correctAnswer: Excludes patterns (test files, helpers, __pycache__) from the scheduler's DAG file scan
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: scheduler scans dags/ for .py files. .airflowignore prevents scanning helpers, test files, __init__.py — anything that is not a DAG.

questions.question: What is SaulėGrid's metadata database size target, and why?
options: Under 5GB — operational state, not analytics data; large DBs slow every scheduler query
Over 100GB — comprehensive history of every DAG Run since the platform was first deployed
Exactly 10GB — the optimal size that PostgreSQL's query planner is tuned for at default settings
Under 100MB — the metadata DB should fit in RAM for the scheduler to access it instantly
questions.correctAnswer: Under 5GB — operational state, not analytics data; large DBs slow every scheduler query
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: metadata DB = bookkeeping, not warehouse. SaulėGrid's actual size stabilizes around 3GB after weekly cleanup runs.

questions.question: What does `airflow db clean --clean-before-timestamp` do?
options: Removes records older than the timestamp from dag_run, task_instance, log, xcom, and similar tables
Drops the entire metadata database and recreates the schema from scratch with default content
Compacts the metadata database by rebuilding all indexes without deleting any historical data
Cleans the worker filesystems by removing log files older than the timestamp from each host
questions.correctAnswer: Removes records older than the timestamp from dag_run, task_instance, log, xcom, and similar tables
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: db clean removes stale operational records. Weekly maintenance DAG runs it to keep the metadata DB lean and fast.

questions.question: An engineer increases `parallelism` to fix 'tasks are queued too long.' Why is this often wrong?
options: If the bottleneck is pool slots or worker capacity, parallelism does nothing — diagnose first
Parallelism is read-only and the engineer's change is silently ignored on the next scheduler restart
Parallelism only affects sub-DAGs and modern Airflow has no sub-DAGs since they were deprecated
Parallelism caps at 32 in the open-source edition — only the commercial edition allows higher values
questions.correctAnswer: If the bottleneck is pool slots or worker capacity, parallelism does nothing — diagnose first
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: increase the binding constraint. Pool slots? executor capacity? parallelism cap? Each has its own fix. Wrong dial = no improvement or worse.

questions.question: Why is heavy data processing inside a PythonOperator an anti-pattern at scale?
options: Worker slots are orchestration resources — every minute of processing is a minute slots cannot dispatch
Heavy processing crashes the worker because Airflow caps task memory at 512 MB by configuration
The metadata database stores intermediate processing results which slows every scheduler query
Heavy processing locks the executor's queue and prevents other tasks from being dispatched at all
questions.correctAnswer: Worker slots are orchestration resources — every minute of processing is a minute slots cannot dispatch
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: thin tasks dispatch fast, freeing slots. Trigger Spark/dbt/SQL — let the processing engine do the heavy work, not the worker.

questions.question: What is SaulėGrid's metrics-and-alerting pipeline?
options: Airflow → StatsD → Prometheus → Grafana — with thresholds defined per panel
Airflow → CloudWatch → AWS X-Ray → Honeycomb — for distributed tracing per request
Airflow → Loki → Datadog APM → PagerDuty — log-based alerting with no metrics layer
Airflow → Splunk → Splunk → Splunk — single-vendor observability across the entire stack
questions.correctAnswer: Airflow → StatsD → Prometheus → Grafana — with thresholds defined per panel
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: StatsD emit, Prometheus scrape, Grafana display. Each panel has owner, threshold, action — anything else is visual noise.

questions.question: Lesson 10 says performance tuning is not about making Airflow 'faster'. What is it about?
options: Identifying which component is the binding constraint and applying the targeted fix verified by metrics
Replacing components piece by piece until the new architecture matches the team's growth projections
Buying enterprise-edition Airflow licenses to unlock features that the open-source edition lacks
Doubling worker counts every quarter regardless of measurement — capacity solves most problems
questions.correctAnswer: Identifying which component is the binding constraint and applying the targeted fix verified by metrics
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: tuning = match the fix to the constraint. Scheduler? DB? Executor? Pool? Each has its own dial.

questions.question: Lesson 1 describes the 'production mindset.' What does it warn happens to platforms that are not actively operated?
options: Configuration drifts, security controls weaken, performance thresholds relax, runbooks rot
Adoption grows uncontrollably until the platform serves more teams than it can support
Cost decreases steadily as workloads stabilize and the cloud bill compresses over time
Performance improves spontaneously as traffic patterns simplify around fewer use cases
questions.correctAnswer: Configuration drifts, security controls weaken, performance thresholds relax, runbooks rot
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: active operations is the immune system. Without it, drift is the natural state — config, security, performance, documentation all decay.

questions.question: What does SaulėGrid require for every metric on the monitoring dashboard?
options: An owner (who investigates), a threshold (when it alerts), and an action (what to do)
A 30-day retention window, a percentile breakdown, and a vendor-supplied performance baseline
A weekly review meeting, a quarterly capacity plan, and an annual budget for tooling upgrades
A markdown comment in the source dashboard JSON explaining the metric in three sentences
questions.correctAnswer: An owner (who investigates), a threshold (when it alerts), and an action (what to do)
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: a metric without owner+threshold+action is visual noise. Quarterly review removes panels that have never alerted.

questions.question: What is the principle behind SaulėGrid's blameless post-mortems?
options: Focus on system failures, not human failures — the system should have prevented the error
Focus on the engineer who caused the incident — accountability requires naming the responsible party
Focus on the customer impact — every post-mortem is published externally to demonstrate transparency
Focus on the cost — every post-mortem includes a financial impact analysis approved by finance
questions.correctAnswer: Focus on system failures, not human failures — the system should have prevented the error
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: the system should prevent errors. If a person caused the incident, ask why the pipeline allowed it. Blame culture hides incidents and prevents learning.

questions.question: What is SaulėGrid's runbook principle?
options: If an operation has been performed more than twice, it should have a runbook anyone can follow
Every team member writes their own personal runbook in their notes app — never shared with peers
Runbooks are a sign of poor automation — every documented procedure should be replaced with a script
Runbooks are read-only documents written once at platform launch and never modified afterward
questions.correctAnswer: If an operation has been performed more than twice, it should have a runbook anyone can follow
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: third occurrence = document. Runbooks eliminate bus factor and let any team member perform any operation reliably.

questions.question: What two on-call anti-patterns does Lesson 5 warn against?
options: Alert fatigue (so many alerts each one gets dismissed) and hero culture (one engineer handles everything)
Pager rotation (engineers swap shifts during an incident) and remote-only on-call (no in-office presence)
Manual escalation (no automatic paging) and silent escalation (alerts go to a channel no one watches)
External vendor on-call (a third party fields alerts) and AI-driven on-call (LLM triages incidents)
questions.correctAnswer: Alert fatigue (so many alerts each one gets dismissed) and hero culture (one engineer handles everything)
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: alert fatigue = real incidents dismissed. Hero culture = burnout, knowledge silos, single point of failure. Both prevented by discipline + rotation.

questions.question: What does SaulėGrid's SLA governance principle treat SLAs as?
options: Contracts between teams — engineering delivers within window, consumers query only after window
Aspirational targets — engineering aims for the SLA but no consequences attach to misses or breaches
Marketing claims — the SLA is published on the platform website but rarely measured internally
Bonus triggers — engineers get a quarterly bonus when SLAs are met four months in a row
questions.correctAnswer: Contracts between teams — engineering delivers within window, consumers query only after window
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: contract = mutual commitment. Engineering on time + consumers wait. Querying before SLA is outside the agreement, not the team's failure.

questions.question: Lesson 7 says platform health metrics serve two audiences. Which two?
options: Engineers (need detail for diagnosis) and stakeholders (need aggregate indicators for decisions)
Developers (need API specifications) and end-users (need product documentation and tutorials)
Auditors (need compliance reports) and customers (need uptime guarantees in their contracts)
Vendors (need usage statistics) and analysts (need raw data exports for their research)
questions.correctAnswer: Engineers (need detail for diagnosis) and stakeholders (need aggregate indicators for decisions)
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: same data, different abstraction. Dashboard = real-time detail for engineers. Health score + monthly report = aggregate for stakeholders.

questions.question: Lesson 8's change management principle says every change must be three things. Which list?
options: Reversible (rollback plan), communicated (notify downstream), traceable (full audit chain)
Tested in staging, deployed to production, and verified by a customer-facing feature flag
Backwards-compatible, performance-neutral, and accompanied by a regulatory impact assessment
Tagged in Git, documented in Confluence, and announced in Slack with a thread per change
questions.correctAnswer: Reversible (rollback plan), communicated (notify downstream), traceable (full audit chain)
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: reversible + communicated + traceable = predictable evolution. Issues trace back to the change that caused them.

questions.question: Lesson 9 says every mid-level pattern 'breaks at enterprise scale.' What does the senior track teach?
options: DAG factories for consistency, namespace isolation for access control, automated governance for change
Microservices architecture, event sourcing, CQRS, and saga patterns adapted to data orchestration
Custom Airflow forks, proprietary executors, hand-tuned schedulers, and bespoke metadata schemas
Manual operations, individual heroics, untested deployments, and YOLO production changes only
questions.correctAnswer: DAG factories for consistency, namespace isolation for access control, automated governance for change
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: senior = patterns that restore order at enterprise scale. Mid-level operates a platform; senior designs the platform others operate.

questions.question: Lesson 10 describes the journey from 'DAG author' to 'platform engineer.' What does the platform engineer now know how to do?
options: Build, test, deploy, secure, tune, and operate a production orchestration platform three teams trust
Write DAGs that run reliably in development on a single machine using LocalExecutor and SQLite
Configure SSH access on the production scheduler so engineers can debug issues by tailing log files
Translate cron expressions into preset strings so DAGs use @daily and @hourly wherever possible
questions.correctAnswer: Build, test, deploy, secure, tune, and operate a production orchestration platform three teams trust
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: the journey covered architecture, design, runtime, secrets, testing, deployment, security, performance, operations. The toolkit is complete.
